reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David expressed strong disagreement with the patch and requested a NACK due to concerns that the patch does not address his previous comments about not reimplementing GUP entirely.
reviews.raw_body:
You mean, ignoring my comments about not reiplementing GUP entirely?

NAK

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed frustration that the patch author did not respond to their review, but did not provide any technical feedback or suggestions.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes pointed out that the patch's description of munmap() behavior is incorrect, as it acquires the VMA lock and removes upper-level tables only if the entire range is spanned. Additionally, PTE page tables can be removed via MADV_DONTNEED under RCU, making the GUP fast imitating code unnecessary.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes noted that the patch does not properly handle the transition from per-VMA locks to RCU, specifically pointing out that retract_page_tables() and try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() still use pte_free_defer(), which should be replaced with RCU-based functions

Reviewer questioned the purpose of the patch, suggesting they may be misunderstanding its intent or that there's a lack of context.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed frustration with the formatting of the patch description, requesting that any AI-generated text be edited for readability.
reviews.raw_body:
Rik, it really would have helped if you'd replied to review :)

On Wed, Jun 24, 2026 at 09:50:52PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:

---

Yeah I need to update the documentation on this at
https://docs.kernel.org/mm/process_addrs.html it's more subtle than written
there.

Firstly you're wrong about munmap() - it acquires the VMA lock of the VMAs freed
in the range and will only remove an upper level table if the entire range is
spanned.

And that's the only way higher level tables can be removed.

PTE page tables can be removed via MADV_DONTNEED, but that a. acquires the VMA
lock and b. frees the PTE page table under RCU.

A THP collapse can happen concurrently, but PTEs are freed under RCU so you
don't need to do this GUP fast imitating stuff.

---

retract_page_tables() -> pte_free_defer() -> RCU
try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() -> pte_free_defer() -> RCU

---

Yes it is?

I mean unless I'm missing something here.

---

This is an unreadable wall of text, if it's AI generated please edit before
sending.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed disagreement with the patch, suggesting that a discussion is needed in the review itself and implying that the patch may not be necessary.

reviewer requested that the patch author provide a more detailed explanation of how the per-VMA lock is used in __access_remote_vm and how it interacts with other locks, specifically mentioning the need for a clear description of the locking hierarchy
reviews.raw_body:
As I said in reply to 2/3 I don't think you need to do this.

Let's have a discussion _in the review_ about it :)

---

No wall of text please :)

Same comments for the rest, please use paragraphs, try to be succinct, etc.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch series should be sent after the merge window closes and not during it

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed dissatisfaction with the patch description, stating that it does not clearly attribute changes to specific reviewers and implying that the author has not adequately addressed previous comments.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed agreement with the patch but emphasized the importance of responding to reviews inline and having discussions to ensure progress is made correctly, as he has limited review time and prefers triage by discussion rather than dismissal.
reviews.raw_body:
(Note that it's the merge window, really ideally you should be saving sending
non-RFC series until after the merge window closes, none of these series will be
taken yet and you'll have to resend anyway)

On Thu, Jun 25, 2026 at 08:32:43AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

---

This is really not sufficient :) you should break out the changes you
make and who suggested them.

If people give their time to review, you should take the time to make it clear
you've dealt with it.

---

Yeah agreed.

As replied elsewhere, I don't think we need to do that anyway?

But you _really_ need to respond to review inline so we can have those
discussions and ensure that you're moving forward in the correct way.

Just quietly resending with a catch-all comment as per above puts all the load
on us to go and check that you've done what we've asked.

On my side, my review time is very limited now, so triage dictates general
dismissals when the series looks wrong, engaging in discussion will help us all
move forward efficiently.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the reliability of system monitoring under mmap_lock contention, specifically during THP collapse. They acknowledge that relying only on the RCU read lock to read the page table could result in seeing old page table contents that no longer point at the current process memory. The author suggests two possible ways for khugepaged to fix this issue and notes that it currently uses the first approach.
reviews.raw_body:
One issue here is that while we can safely read
the old page table under the RCU read lock, in
the middle of a THP collapse there is no guarantee
that the old page table points at the process's 
current memory.

Khugepaged could fix this in one of two ways:
- zap all readers with an IPI, and use that as
  synchronization
- make sure the old page table's PTEs point at
  the individual pages inside the new PMD

Right now khugepaged does the first.

Relying only on the RCU read lock to read the
page table could result in us seeing old page
table contents, that no longer point at the
current process memory.

Unless I'm missing something...

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about the feasibility of implementing a mmap_lock-less GUP, expressed willingness to work on it but wants to understand prerequisites and acceptable implementation before proceeding.
reviews.raw_body:
Do we actually have a path to doing that?

I misread that as more of a wish list thing, not
as something we could realistically do today.

How would I go about making that mmap_lock-less
GUP a reality?

What are the prerequisites?

I'm not opposed to working on that, but I
would like to figure out ahead of time
what an acceptable implementation would
roughly look like.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-06-25.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a fast path for __access_remote_vm that uses per-VMA locks instead of the process-wide mmap_lock, improving system monitoring reliability and speeding up the function under contention. The fast path is only enabled when it's safe to do so, requiring a kernel with per-VMA locks, RCU page table freeing, and specific VMA types excluded. This change aims to address issues where processes get stuck holding the mmap_lock for extended periods, hindering system monitoring tools' ability to assess and log situations.

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) expressed concerns about the patch's potential to introduce unnecessary complexity, specifically in GUP users, and requested that alternatives be explored before proceeding.

Reviewer David (Arm) expressed confusion and skepticism about the patch's intention to add a fast path for __access_remote_vm under mmap_lock contention, questioning how the current implementation aligns with the stated goal of not doing something 'unless inevitable'.

Reviewer David expressed frustration and disappointment with the patch's reimplementation of a GUP-like interface, suggesting that it is not necessary and may be overly complex. He requests someone with GUP experience to properly rethink the lookup+fault path instead of adding workarounds.
reviews.raw_body:
Well, I know what we do not want to reimplement GUP in some GUP users unless
really unavoidable. And I want that explored first before moving further into
this growing complexity in a GUP users.

---

I really don't know how you concluded that.

I mean, I know, my English is bad, but it's hard for me to get how

	"Yeah, we're not doing that unless inevitable."

is a wish list.

Yeah, well, ok, my kids treat also a "no" as a wish list ;)

---

Did you actually read at least some of the stuff I wrote in my mails? You know,
the ones where you didn't reply to? :)

Seeing a v2 with some weird gup-fast-like looking reimplementatiom (eww) is not
something I enjoyed at this point. We don't need another set of special page
table walkers.

We really need someone to look into this with some GUP experience or the
willingness to properly think the GUP lookup+fault path through, instead of
adding some creative workarounds to selective GUP user.

I will try to find some time to think it through, but my time would be better
spent guiding someone (and definitely not someones LLM) to understand

(1) which interface we could start with (as I said, a GUP interface where we
pass a VMA-lcoked / mm-read-lcoked VMA instead of the MM)

(2) which faults we could automatically resolve under VMA lock (I mentioned
userfaultfd is tricky but the existing GUP call already doesn't trigger uffd)

(3) whether gup-fast could be reused to some degree, or what it would take in
order to do that.

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author identified __access_remote_vm as a suitable starting point for implementing per-VMA locks, citing its pain points and high caller count.

Author addressed David's concern that normal pages could be accessed using only per-VMA locks, proposing to implement a locking function for __access_remote_vm() that takes the appropriate lock and handles expand_stack() calls. The author suggests taking the slow path for accesses spanning multiple VMAs and adjusting locking assertions in related functions.

Author is addressing David's (Arm) feedback on whether __access_remote_vm can use gup-fast for accesses entirely within a single VMA, and considering switching to gup-fast as an alternative to eliding the first VMA lookup in get_user_pages. Author acknowledges that this makes things clearer but asks if there are any major issues overlooked.
sentiment_signals: focusing on one path at a time
reviews.raw_body:
__access_remote_vm seems like a decent place to start,
since that is a pain point for several people, and also
the path into remote get_user_pages with the most callers.

After that there are a few more callers in performance
sensitive paths:
- execve / argv setup -> new mm, no lock contention?
- KVM async pagefault path -> most/all of these to the same VMA?
- make_device_exclusive -> ???
- pin_user_pages_remote -> probably a good target?

---

It looks like for normal pages, any access that
does not require expanding the stack could be
done using just the per-VMA locks.

That might allow us to implement a locking
function for __access_remote_vm() that
takes the appropriate lock for the situation,
and also takes care of the expand_stack() 
calls, if needed.

We could also take the slow path for an
access that spans multiple VMAs.

Then we could have an inner loop that does
only the page accessing and copying, with
no calls to expand_stack() in the while (len)
loop.

At the end of __access_remote_vm() we can
then unlock according to the way we locked.

Adjusting the locking assertions in __get_user_pages_locked
and untagged_addr_remote to allow the per-VMA lock seem
fairly straightforward.

Passing the starting vma all the way into __get_user_pages
would also allow us to skip looking up the VMA a second
time after the first lookup in __access_remote_vm().

---

If an access is entirely inside a single VMA,
it looks like using gup-fast could be possible?

The whole "look up VMA permissions" thing inside
get_user_pages() should not be necessary for callers
like __access_remote_vm() that have already done
that.

That could be a nice speedup for thing like
ptrace, BPF copy_from_user_task, etc

Another question is whether switching right
to gup-fast would be cleaner than trying to
elide the first VMA lookup from __get_user_pages,
but answering that may require looking at some
prototype code and figuring out more of the
details.

Thank you for asking the questions above.
Looking into those made things a little
clearer.

This looks a whole lot more manageable now.

Are there any major things I overlooked?

Are there any pending patches in -mm or
elsewhere that I should pull into my tree
before starting?

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a fast path for __access_remote_vm that uses per-VMA locks instead of the process-wide mmap_lock, improving system monitoring reliability and speeding up the function under contention. The fast path is only enabled when it's safe to do so, requiring a kernel with per-VMA locks, RCU page table freeing, and specific VMA types excluded. This change aims to address issues where processes get stuck holding the mmap_lock for extended periods, hindering system monitoring tools' ability to assess and log situations.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer emphasized the importance of engaging with reviewers during the review process to ensure that all comments and concerns are addressed before proceeding with further changes.
sentiment_signals: reviewer emphasized the importance of engagement
reviewer highlighted potential consequences of not addressing comments
reviews.raw_body:
I agree with everything David said here.

In general Rik do make sure to engage with reviewers during the review and reply
to points raised so we can have a healthy discussion (I see you replied to me
thanks for that! So hopefully this is now resolved).

It'll save you going off and doing X, Y + Z in contradiction of comments you
perhaps missed or felt were nice-to-haves, only to find that they
were in fact critical blockers that prevent your series from being merged.

With review workload as it is this not only saves you time, stress + energy, but
saves your reviewers from the same :)

Generally if there's feedback, response to all of it is expected and really
required, albeit obviously for reasons of economy an aggregrate response or
implied acks on nits are fine.

Thanks, Lorenzo

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer asked the submitter to include a diffstat in the cover letter and adjust their tooling to do so, noting that git format-patch --cover-letter already does this by default.
reviews.raw_body:
Please always send a diffstat in your cover letter by the way.

I noticed you didn't do this elsewhere also, so please adjust your tooling to do
this :) (git format-patch --cover-letter does this by default).

Thanks, Lorenzo

The fast path is only enabled when it's safe to do so, requiring a kernel with per-VMA locks, RCU page table freeing, and specific VMA types excluded. This change aims to address issues where processes get stuck holding the mmap_lock for extended periods, hindering system monitoring tools' ability to assess and log situations.
